text.title: Serverless Cold Start Optimization Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the serverless-cold-start-optimization methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with sourced rationale; the final rule is the skip-this-methodology gate referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: Mitigation work MUST start from a measured baseline (cold + warm p50 / p95 / p99 at expected RPS). Cold-start fixes without baseline are speculative and often regress warm latency or cost.

rationale.source: AWS Lambda performance whitepapers; cold-start postmortems.

rule.rationale: Cold-start optimisation has knobs that hurt cost (PC) or warm latency (memory bloat); evidence-based picks avoid regressions.

rule.statement: Mitigation MUST be tried cheapest first: bundle trim + import lazy-loading → memory bump → runtime swap (Node/Python > Java) → SnapStart → provisioned concurrency → container migration. Skipping cheap options to apply PC by default wastes budget.
rule.rationale: Cheaper mitigations frequently close the gap; PC costs $0.0000041/GB-s × seconds; defaulting to PC mints unnecessary cost.

rule.statement: When runtime is replaceable, prefer runtimes with shorter cold starts: Node.js / Python / Go < Rust < .NET < Java (without SnapStart). Java + SnapStart approaches Node/Python class.
rule.rationale: Runtime is the single biggest determinant of cold-start latency; switching can shave seconds.

rule.statement: Provisioned concurrency MUST be sized to expected RPS / concurrency, not as a 'set it and forget it' constant. Auto-scaling on PC MUST be configured to avoid cold spikes during traffic ramps.

rationale.source: AWS Application Auto Scaling for Lambda PC.

rule.rationale: Under-provisioned PC still cold-starts during spikes; over-provisioned PC pays for idle capacity.

rule.statement: Post-fix numbers MUST be measured at the same RPS as the baseline; the spec MUST document the % cold-start reduction AND any warm-latency / cost change.
rule.rationale: Without post-measurement, the mitigation cannot be defended or rolled back; regressions are common (warm latency up after memory bump).

rule.statement: When prerequisites hold (latency budget, baseline, runtime config, bundle data), apply r1 → r5 and emit the spec.
rule.rationale: Order encodes risk: measure first, cheap fix second, runtime third, PC sizing fourth, validate last.

rule.statement: Do NOT use when (a) async non-user-facing function, (b) steady high-RPS, (c) SLO already met. Exit with note.
rule.rationale: Cold-start ceremony in these contexts wastes engineering capacity.
